pattern: Overlap glues ranges into connected clusters that must always land in the same group, so once you merge every chain of overlaps into independent clusters, each cluster can freely go to either group and the count is a power of two.
hints: Two ranges that overlap must share a group, and that requirement is transitive — if A overlaps B and B overlaps C, all three are forced together even if A and C don't touch directly.
Sort the ranges by their start and sweep left to right, merging any range into the current cluster whenever its start falls at or before the cluster's current rightmost endpoint.
Every finished, independent cluster can be assigned to either of the two groups with no further constraint, so if there are c clusters the answer is 2^c, taken modulo 1e9+7.

edge_inputs.description: single range, one cluster, two ways
two disjoint ranges, two independent clusters
ranges only touch at a single shared endpoint, which still counts as overlapping
a chain of three ranges where the first and last never touch directly but are still forced into one cluster
one range fully nested inside another
ranges spanning the full value range with a single point range at the maximum
many disjoint single-point ranges, every one its own cluster
ranges given out of sorted order still merge correctly once sorted internally

stress.description: many small ranges scattered across a wide domain, some overlapping, some not
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = min(100000, max(1, size))
    ranges = []
    for _ in range(n):
        s = rng.randint(0, 1000000000 - 20)
        e = s + rng.randint(0, 20)
        ranges.append([s, e])
    return [ranges]
